stories.headline: Colorado Appeals Court Overturns Tina Peters' Prison Sentence, Upholds Conviction
stories.summary:
The Colorado Court of Appeals on Thursday overturned the nine-year prison sentence handed to former Mesa County Clerk Tina Peters, ordering a lower court to re-evaluate her punishment. A three-judge panel upheld her 2024 conviction for orchestrating a security breach of her county's election system in 2021, but found the sentence was imposed in part on improper grounds.

In a 77-page opinion, the panel concluded that sentencing Judge Matthew Barrett had factored in Peters' continued public statements about election fraud, effectively punishing her for protected speech. The judges wrote that the tenor of Barrett's comments made clear he felt the lengthy sentence was necessary, at least in part, to prevent Peters from continuing to espouse views he deemed damaging — but that Peters is no longer a county clerk and thus no longer in a position to repeat the conduct underlying her conviction.

Attorney General Phil Weiser called Peters' original sentence fair and appropriate, noting she has shown no remorse. Peters' attorney Peter Ticktin called the decision 'terribly disappointing,' saying the court 'kicked the can down the road' rather than granting a new trial. Peters has been incarcerated for 549 days.

District Attorney Dan Rubinstein said the ruling underscored the integrity of the judicial process, noting the court reviewed nine claims of error and found no deficiencies in the trial or conviction. The case will return to the 21st Judicial District for resentencing.

stories.headline: Federal Judge Rules DPS 'Opened the Door for a Mass Shooting' at East High, Allows Lawsuit to Proceed
stories.summary:
A federal judge has allowed a lawsuit brought by former East High School dean Eric Sinclair against Denver Public Schools to move forward, writing that district officials 'seem to have knowingly opened the door for a mass shooting' at the school. U.S. District Judge Gordon Gallagher said DPS exhibited a 'shocking disregard' for the risk posed by student Austin Lyle, who shot Sinclair and another administrator in March 2023 before fleeing and dying by suicide.

Court documents detail that Lyle had previously been expelled from Overland High School in Cherry Creek after police found an AR-15, loaded magazines, ammunition and a silencer in his bedroom. East High placed Lyle on a safety plan requiring daily check-ins and backpack searches when he enrolled. On the day of the shooting, Sinclair was searching Lyle's bag when the student guided the dean's hand to a gun concealed in his hoodie, then opened fire — striking Sinclair in the thigh and through the stomach and chest, resulting in the loss of his spleen.

DPS responded that the judge's order does not constitute a finding that the district violated the law, and that at this stage the court must accept the plaintiff's allegations as fact. The district said it is confident the evidence will show its actions were legally consistent and said it looks forward to presenting its case in court.

Sinclair filed suit last year alleging staff were inadequately trained and that the district failed to act on clear warning signs. The ruling allows his claims to be tested in court.

stories.headline: Denver Judge Denies Gov. Polis' Bid to Dismiss ICE Records Lawsuit
stories.summary:
A Denver judge has denied Gov. Jared Polis' request to dismiss a lawsuit over his 2025 decision to block the release of state labor records to U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. The lawsuit, originally filed by former Colorado Department of Labor official Scott Moss, challenged Polis over an ICE administrative subpoena seeking data on 35 individuals who are custodians of undocumented unaccompanied minor children.

Judge Jones had previously issued a temporary injunction in June 2025 barring compliance with the subpoena, agreeing that doing so would violate state laws enacted in 2021 and 2024. In December, Polis sought a permanent injunction and final judgment on his own terms — but the judge ruled Monday that the motion amounted to a 'hybrid confession of judgment' and an offer of settlement, both on the governor's terms, which the plaintiff had not accepted.

The court noted that partial relief would be inconsistent with Polis' stated goal of fully resolving the case, and that the motion failed to identify the controlling legal question. The governor's office did not respond to a request for comment.

ICE has never moved to enforce the subpoena in court. Moss, who left the Department of Labor last August, has returned to teaching at the CU law school. The case continues.

stories.headline: One Person Killed in Shooting Near East Denver Condo Complex
stories.summary:
One person died Thursday after a shooting near a condo complex in east Denver, according to the Denver Police Department. Officers responded to the 10100 block of East Virginia Avenue just before 1 p.m. and located a victim who was transported to a hospital and later pronounced dead.

Denver police are investigating the incident as a homicide. No suspect information or circumstances surrounding the shooting have been released. Anyone with information is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Denver Police Seek Suspect in Sexual Assault Aboard RTD Light Rail Train
stories.summary:
Denver police are asking the public for help identifying a man suspected of committing a sexual assault on an RTD light rail train in late March. The incident occurred around 11:05 a.m. on March 21 on a train leaving the Perry Street Station, according to a Metro Denver Crime Stoppers bulletin.

The suspect is described as a Black male between 30 and 50 years old, approximately 5 feet 10 inches tall, with a thin build and short hair. He was last seen wearing a green and white sweatshirt and jeans. No additional details about the nature of the assault or the victim were provided.

Anyone with information is asked to contact Crime Stoppers at (720) 913-7867 or submit a tip online.

stories.headline: Colorado Home Insurance Premiums Soaring Faster Than Any Other State
stories.summary:
Colorado homeowners are facing the steepest home insurance premium increases in the nation, with statewide costs doubling since 2020 and rising 55 percent since 2023. On average, Colorado residents are now spending nearly $1 out of every $22 earned insuring their homes, according to a Denver Post analysis.

The surge is being driven by a combination of factors including severe hail and wind storms, wildfire risk, and what industry observers say is a pattern of opportunistic roof replacement claims following hailstorms. Weld County leads the nation in tornado touchdowns since 1950, and Colorado ranks second behind Texas in hail-related insurance claims.

Some homeowners are seeing extreme cases: Peter McClure of Severance watched his annual premium rise from $1,584 in 2021 to a quote of $11,300 from Nationwide — a sevenfold increase — before finding a policy from Farmers for $5,404. Denver-area insurance broker Dustin Hailey said annual increases of $700 to $1,000 per policy are now commonplace, with deductibles for hail and wind damage also climbing.

The insurance cost spike compounds an already difficult financial environment for Colorado homeowners who have also faced sharp property tax increases in recent years. Industry observers warn the trend shows no sign of reversing given the escalating frequency and intensity of severe weather events across the Front Range.

stories.headline: Speed Camera Enforcement Begins on I-25; Denver Plans Fixed Cameras on Federal, Alameda
stories.summary:
Drivers in the Denver metro area face increased speed camera enforcement starting this week as state and local authorities expand automated traffic surveillance. The Colorado Department of Transportation's speed cameras on I-25 north of metro Denver between Mead and Berthoud have captured more than 4,000 speeding drivers since March 1; the warning period has ended and violators will now receive $75 citations.

Denver is also planning its first fixed speed cameras along high-accident stretches of Alameda Avenue and Federal Boulevard, expected to be installed later this year. Since January 2024, four city photo radar vans have already issued citations to more than 258,000 drivers. A Denver City Council ordinance update is still required before fixed cameras can be deployed.

Traffic deaths in Denver reached a record 93 last year, up 89 percent from 49 in 2017. City records show Federal Boulevard logged 643 crashes in 2023, including six fatalities, while Alameda saw 478 crashes and two deaths. At least 27 cities and towns statewide have now authorized automated speed camera enforcement.

Colorado State Patrol, which issued more than 14,500 tickets in 2025 to drivers going 20 to 40 mph over the limit, says the cameras are a necessary force multiplier for a 700-trooper agency that cannot cover every road. Kevin Bommer of the Colorado Municipal League said the cost of speeding to life and property is dramatic and traditional enforcement alone cannot address it.

stories.headline: Seven More Colorado Ski Resorts to Close Sunday as Season Winds Down
stories.summary:
Seven Colorado ski resorts will close for the season on Sunday, leaving only seven areas still operating statewide. Among those shutting down Sunday are Keystone, Steamboat, Snowmass, Crested Butte, Eldora and Wolf Creek, with Telluride also closing after briefly reopening Friday following an earlier closure.

Most resorts are operating on severely reduced terrain, with Steamboat at just 9 percent, Keystone at 14 percent and Crested Butte at 19 percent. Wolf Creek, closing with 90 percent of its terrain open, fared best thanks to 19 inches of new snow this week. Crested Butte received 22 inches from the same storm.

After Sunday, the remaining open areas will be Loveland, Aspen Mountain, Copper Mountain, Breckenridge, Vail, Winter Park and Arapahoe Basin, all operating with limited terrain ranging from 8 to 33 percent. Vail and Aspen are scheduled to close April 19, while Copper Mountain targets April 26. Breckenridge, Arapahoe Basin, Winter Park and Loveland plan to stay open as long as conditions allow.

The early closures reflect a difficult season marked by Colorado's lowest snowpack on record during the 2025-26 winter and historically warm temperatures across the Front Range.

stories.headline: RTD to Disrupt Light Rail Service to Union Station April 11-13 for Wire Replacement
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District will interrupt light rail service to and from Denver Union Station on the E and W lines over the weekend of April 11-13 to replace approximately 1,600 feet of overhead electrical wire. Regular service on all lines will resume Tuesday, April 14.

During the outage, E Line trains will be rerouted to the Downtown Loop rather than Union Station, stopping at Colfax at Auraria and 16th and 18th street stations. Temporary bus shuttle service will connect passengers to Union Station from 10th-Osage Station. W Line riders will be served by bus shuttles between Auraria West Station and Union Station.

RTD said the work is part of its Building a Better RTD strategic initiative, which emphasizes proactive infrastructure maintenance to improve system reliability. Customers are advised to use the agency's Next Ride app to plan alternate routes and monitor service alerts.

stories.headline: Imperial Chinese Restaurant Closes After 41 Years in Denver's Baker Neighborhood
stories.summary:
Imperial Chinese Restaurant, a long-standing fixture in Denver's Baker neighborhood, has closed after 41 years in operation. The closure was described as unexpected, according to the Denver Business Journal.

The restaurant's decades-long run made it one of the more enduring dining establishments in the neighborhood. No further details about the circumstances of the closure were immediately available.

stories.headline: Tallgrass Energy Names New CEO; Polis Appoints Leaders to State Health Agencies
stories.summary:
Crystal Heter has been appointed chief executive officer of Tallgrass Energy, the Denver-area natural gas pipeline company, effective March 27. The leadership transition marks a significant change at one of the region's prominent energy infrastructure firms.

Separately, Gov. Jared Polis appointed Gretchen Hammer and Stephanie Beasley to lead Colorado's health care agencies following recent departures. The appointments come amid ongoing scrutiny of the state's Medicaid program and broader health policy landscape.

stories.headline: Rockies Open Home Schedule Friday Against Phillies; RTD Adds Rail Capacity for Opener
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies open their 2026 home schedule Friday at Coors Field against the Philadelphia Phillies, with first pitch set for 2:10 p.m. The six-game homestand runs through April 8 and marks the team's return to Blake Street following a road-heavy start to the season.

RTD is boosting capacity on the D, E and W light rail lines through the weekend to accommodate anticipated higher ridership. The agency will also host a block party with its Impact Team from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. Friday at Union Station, with staff assisting fans with wayfinding, ticket purchases and trip planning.

Riders are advised to use RTD's Next Ride app and enter 'Coors Field' or 'Rockies' as the destination for real-time transit options. Bus routes serving the stadium area include the 120X, 38, 52 and RX lines at stops near Park Avenue West, Wewatta Street and Larimer and Market streets.

weather.notable: Nearly 8 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I grew up in a small town. It was so small, the school bully had to outsource.

weekendEvents.title: Colorado Rockies Home Opener vs. Philadelphia Phillies
weekendEvents.description: The Colorado Rockies open their 2026 home season against the Philadelphia Phillies. First pitch Friday is at 2:10 p.m., with Saturday at 6:10 p.m. and Sunday at 1:10 p.m. Promotional magnet schedules distributed to all fans on Opening Day.
weekendEvents.time: Fri 2:10 p.m., Sat 6:10 p.m., Sun 1:10 p.m.

weekendEvents.title: Opening Day Party at McGregor Square
weekendEvents.description: Free all-day celebration of Rockies Opening Day at McGregor Square, with the bar open at 9 a.m. and a DJ starting at 10:30 a.m. Fans without game tickets can watch on the 66-foot Jumbotron.

weekendEvents.title: First Friday Art Walk
weekendEvents.description: Denver's monthly First Friday Art Walk takes place across five art districts, including the Arts District on Santa Fe, the Golden Triangle Museum District, Tennyson Street Cultural District, and the RiNo Arts District.

weekendEvents.title: Red Bull BC One Cypher Denver
weekendEvents.description: Top break dancers from across the region compete in a 1v1 showdown, with the top three B-Boys and top three B-Girls advancing to the Red Bull BC One USA National Final in San Diego.
weekendEvents.location: Boettcher Concert Hall, Denver Performing Arts Complex, 100 14th Street, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Family Egg Hunt at Four Mile Historic Park
weekendEvents.description: Four Mile Historic Park hosts its annual egg hunt with over 30,000 eggs, age-specific hunts including a new adult-only hunt, a magic show, live music, Easter Bunny meet and greet, tractor rides, croquet, egg rolling contests, face painting, and crafts.
weekendEvents.time: 8:30 a.m. to noon and 1 to 5 p.m.
weekendEvents.location: Four Mile Historic Park, 715 South Forest Street, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Collaboration Fest
weekendEvents.description: Brewers from across Colorado team up to pour ultra-unique collaborative beers for attendees to sample. Tickets range from $45 to $110 for general admission.

weekendEvents.title: 88 Drive-In Theatre Season Opener — 50th Anniversary
weekendEvents.description: The only drive-in movie theater in the Denver metro reopens for its 50th summer season with a double feature of 'The Super Mario Galaxy Movie' and 'Reminders of Him.' Outside food is now permitted with a $10 permit. Box office opens at 6:30 p.m.

weekendEvents.title: Troma Night: The Toxic Avenger Double Feature
weekendEvents.description: As part of CU Boulder's International Film Series, this event features a moderated Q&A and screenings of the original 'The Toxic Avenger' (1984) and the 2023 remake. Toxic Avenger creator Lloyd Kaufman will introduce the films remotely, and original cast member Mark Torgl will attend in person.
weekendEvents.location: Muenzinger Auditorium, University of Colorado Boulder, 1905 Colorado Avenue, Boulder

weekendEvents.title: Colorado Avalanche vs. St. Louis Blues
weekendEvents.description: The Colorado Avalanche host the St. Louis Blues at Ball Arena on Sunday evening.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Nuggets vs. San Antonio Spurs
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Nuggets host the San Antonio Spurs at Ball Arena on Sunday afternoon.

weekendEvents.title: Daybreaker Sober Dance Party at Red Rocks
weekendEvents.description: Daybreaker marks its 1,000th worldwide event with its first-ever daytime dance party at Red Rocks Amphitheatre. The event begins with a yoga session at 11 a.m. followed by a two-hour dance party. The event is alcohol-free. Tickets start at $31 for the dance party only or $41 for yoga and dancing.

weekendEvents.title: 79th Annual Red Rocks Easter Sunday Sunrise Service
weekendEvents.description: Thousands gather at Red Rocks Amphitheatre for an outdoor nondenominational church service to celebrate Easter. The service is free and open to the public; no ticket or reservation is required. Seating and parking are available on a first-come, first-served basis.

topStory.headline: Hegseth Fires Army Chief of Staff George, Two Other Generals in Latest Military Purge
topStory.summary:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has ousted Army Chief of Staff Gen. Randy George, ordering him to step down and retire immediately, the Pentagon confirmed Thursday. Two additional Army generals were removed alongside George: Gen. David Hodne, who led the Army's Transformation and Training Command, and Maj. Gen. William Green Jr., who served as chief of the Army Chaplain Corps. The Pentagon offered no reason for any of the departures.

Gen. Christopher LaNeve, who had been serving as Army vice chief of staff and previously worked as Hegseth's personal military aide, will step in as acting Army chief of staff. Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell said LaNeve is "a battle-tested leader with decades of operational experience and is completely trusted by Secretary Hegseth to carry out the vision of this administration without fault." A senior Defense Department official told CBS News that "it was time for a leadership change in the Army."

George, a West Point graduate and career infantry officer, was nominated to the Army's top post by President Joe Biden in 2023 and was nearly three years into what is typically a four-year term set to run through 2027. He deployed across multiple conflicts including Operation Desert Storm, Operation Iraqi Freedom, and Operation Enduring Freedom, and previously served as senior military assistant to former Defense Secretary Lloyd Austin. He had survived Hegseth's initial wave of military firings in February 2025.

The dismissals mark the latest chapter in an unprecedented turnover at the highest levels of the U.S. armed forces since Hegseth took office. More than a dozen senior military leaders have now been removed or forced into early retirement, including Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Gen. Charles Q. Brown Jr., Chief of Naval Operations Adm. Lisa Franchetti, and Air Force Vice Chief of Staff Gen. James Slife. Republican Rep. Rich McCormick of Georgia called George's removal "concerning," telling Newsmax he would "be very curious to hear why" and describing George as "a brilliant mind."

The firings come nearly five weeks into active U.S.-Israeli military operations against Iran, with no clear timeline from President Trump on when the conflict may end. Hodne's Army Transformation and Training Command had only been stood up in December as part of George's effort to modernize the service. LaNeve's rapid ascent — from two-star general just two years ago to acting Army chief of staff — underscores the degree to which Hegseth is reshaping the military's senior leadership with officers seen as aligned with the administration's priorities.
